planting.note: Sow directly outdoors once soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: This native wild sunflower is a vigorous, widely branching annual that can top ten feet, so give it room and a sunny, open spot. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm garden soil after the last frost, thinning seedlings to prevent crowding. The branching habit produces many golden-yellow blooms that feed bees and later provide seed for birds. Stake tall plants in windy sites and water steadily until they are well established.

planting.note: Start indoors a few weeks before last frost or sow directly once soil warms.

directions: Crackerjack is a tall African marigold prized for large, showy double pompons in gold, orange, and yellow on sturdy stems. Start seeds indoors for a head start or sow directly after frost danger passes, barely covering them. Plant in full sun where the strong scent helps deter garden pests, making it a classic vegetable-garden companion. Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming all summer and pinch young plants to encourage branching.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out after all frost danger passes.

directions: This compact dahlia mix grows to about eighteen inches, making it ideal for containers, borders, and small-space gardens. Start seeds indoors under bright light six weeks before the last frost, then move plants outdoors once nights stay warm. The cheerful single blooms come in reds, pinks, yellows, and whites and draw bees and butterflies through summer. Deadhead regularly for continuous color, and in cold zones lift and store the tubers over winter.

planting.note: Start indoors well ahead of last frost; seeds need light to germinate.

directions: This dwarf petunia blend stays compact and covers itself in trumpet-shaped flowers in red, purple, blue, and white. Surface-sow the tiny seeds on moist mix and press them in without covering, since they need light to sprout, and start them indoors a couple months before planting out. Grow in full sun in containers, borders, or hanging baskets and feed regularly for nonstop bloom. Pinch back leggy stems and deadhead to keep the plants tidy and flowering.

planting.note: Sow in cool soil in early spring; soaking seeds overnight aids germination.

directions: This naturally compact sweet pea mix keeps the classic fragrance without needing a tall trellis, so it suits containers and small beds. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil, since sweet peas love chilly starts and dislike summer heat. Provide steady moisture and a sunny spot, and pick blooms often to prolong the flowering season. A short bit of twiggy support or a low fence helps the bushy vines stay upright.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring; self-sows readily in the garden.

directions: Chinese forget-me-not produces soft clouds of tiny sky-blue flowers that suit borders and cottage plantings. Scatter seeds directly on prepared soil in early spring and press them in lightly, keeping the bed evenly moist until seedlings appear. This easy annual tolerates partial shade and often self-sows to return in following seasons. Cut a few stems for small bouquets, and shear lightly after the first flush to encourage more bloom.

planting.note: Sow after frost; soaking hard seeds overnight speeds sprouting.

directions: Four o'clocks earn their name by opening fragrant, trumpet-shaped blooms in the late afternoon and evening in a lively mix of colors. Soak the hard seeds overnight and sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot where the fast-growing bushy plants can fill out. The evening flowers attract moths and hummingbirds, and the plants form tuberous roots that can be lifted and stored in cold regions. In mild zones they return each year and often self-sow.

planting.note: Start indoors for early bloom or direct sow after frost.

directions: This French marigold mix stays compact at about a foot tall with bright, two- to three-inch double blooms in gold, orange, yellow, and red. Sow seeds shallowly after the last frost or start indoors for earlier color, and place in full sun. The tidy mounded plants edge beds neatly and pair well with vegetables to help repel pests. Deadhead often for continuous flowering right up to fall frost.

planting.note: Fall sowing provides the cold stratification the seed needs; spring sowing also works after chilling.

directions: This native prairie perennial sends up tall, upright spikes of vivid purple bottlebrush flowers that bloom from the top down. Seeds germinate best after a cold period, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where the plants are drought tolerant once established and popular with butterflies. The long-lasting spikes make excellent fresh or dried cut flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring where plants are to grow; poppies resent transplanting.

directions: This California poppy carries sunny yellow petals with warm orange centers on wispy, blue-green foliage. Sow seeds directly where they are to grow, since the taproot dislikes being moved, and simply press them into the soil surface in early spring. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained ground, where it thrives with little water once established. It self-sows freely and naturalizes beautifully in meadows and dry banks.

planting.note: Nick and soak seeds, then direct sow once soil is warm.

directions: This beloved heirloom climbs quickly to produce large, velvety purple trumpets marked with a striking star-shaped center. Nick the hard seed coats and soak overnight, then sow directly against a trellis or fence once the soil is warm. Blooms open in the morning and feed pollinators, with heart-shaped foliage covering the support all season. Provide sturdy vertical structure, as vines can reach ten feet or more.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring; tolerates light frost and cool weather.

directions: This rare heirloom calendula opens warm gold, peach, and orange blooms each accented by an unusual lime-green center. Sow seeds directly in early spring, covering lightly since they germinate best in darkness, and thin to give plants room. This cool-season annual tolerates light frost and blooms best before summer heat, so successive sowings extend the show. The edible petals brighten salads and the plant has a long history of medicinal use; deadhead often to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant out in cool spring weather for best bloom.

directions: Hoary stock, also called gillyflower, produces tall stems packed with intensely fragrant blooms in soft shades. Start seeds indoors and set young plants out in cool spring weather, as stock performs best in mild temperatures and dislikes heat. Grow in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and even moisture for the fullest flower spikes. Harvest stems for long-lasting, sweetly scented bouquets.

planting.note: Sow in spring for bloom the following year, or in fall to establish over winter.

directions: This heirloom hollyhock sends up dramatic five- to six-foot spikes lined with large blooms in a mix of colors, perfect for the back of a cottage border. Sow seeds shallowly in spring or fall, keeping in mind that as a biennial it usually flowers the second season. Plant in full sun with support in windy sites, and space generously for good airflow to reduce rust. Allow some seed to drop and it will happily naturalize year after year.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; light aids germination so barely cover seed.

directions: This native perennial coreopsis produces masses of golden-yellow, daisy-like flowers above slender, lance-shaped foliage. Press seeds into the soil surface in spring since light helps them germinate, and keep the bed moist until seedlings establish. It thrives in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, tolerating drought and heat while attracting bees and butterflies. Deadhead to prolong bloom, and let some seed heads mature to feed birds and self-sow.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost once soil warms.

directions: Lemon Queen is a graceful branching heirloom sunflower with soft lemon-yellow petals surrounding dark centers. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm soil after frost, spacing plants to allow each branching stem room to develop. Reaching five to seven feet, it produces many blooms per plant from midsummer on and is a magnet for bees. Provide full sun and steady moisture, and cut stems freely for long-lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; benefits from cool soil to germinate.

directions: Lewis blue flax is a graceful native perennial that opens clear sky-blue flowers each morning atop slender, wiry stems. Sow seeds directly in spring or fall on well-drained soil, pressing them in lightly and keeping the surface moist until they sprout. Once established it is remarkably drought tolerant and thrives in full sun across a wide range of conditions. Allow spent flowers to set seed and it will self-sow to form drifts over time.

planting.note: Soak seeds and sow in cool early-spring soil at the base of a trellis.

directions: Lord Nelson is a richly fragrant heirloom sweet pea with deep, nearly navy-blue blooms on vigorous climbing vines. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil at the foot of a trellis or fence, since sweet peas thrive in chilly starts and fade in heat. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist and pick flowers frequently to keep new buds forming. Provide sturdy vertical support for the five- to six-foot vines.

planting.note: Soak and sow in cool spring soil beside a trellis.

directions: This mammoth sweet pea mix delivers large, fragrant blooms in soft rose, pink, lavender, and white on vigorous vines. Soak seeds overnight and sow in cool early-spring soil against a trellis, since these cottage-garden climbers love a chilly start. Keep roots cool and moisture steady, and harvest often to encourage a long succession of scented flowers. The five- to six-foot vines need sturdy support to climb.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; needs warm soil and full sun.

directions: This giant heirloom sunflower grows on a single stem to ten or twelve feet and crowns it with a massive golden head up to a foot across. Sow seeds an inch deep directly in warm soil after frost, spacing generously so the huge plants have room. Give full sun and steady water, and stake tall stems in exposed sites to keep them from toppling. The abundant seeds are excellent for roasting or feeding birds once heads mature.

planting.note: Fall sowing supplies cold stratification; spring sowing works after chilling the seed.

directions: Marsh mallow is a tall perennial herb with soft gray-green foliage and pale pink flowers, long valued for its soothing roots and leaves. The seed germinates best after a cold period, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before a spring sowing. Give it full sun and consistently moist soil, reflecting its natural home in damp meadows and marshy ground. Plants may be slow the first year but form deep roots that can be harvested from established clumps.

planting.note: Fall sow for natural stratification; cold-treat seed before spring sowing.

directions: This native perennial sunflower towers up to ten feet and lines its stems with golden-yellow blooms in late summer and fall. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring planting, and give it full sun with room to spread since it forms robust colonies. It is drought tolerant once established and provides valuable late-season nectar and seed for pollinators and birds. Cut back or divide clumps periodically to keep the vigorous spread in check.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.

directions: Mexican sunflower is a tall, bushy annual covered in vibrant orange-red daisy blooms that are a favorite of monarch butterflies. Sow seeds directly after frost in warm soil and full sun, thinning to give the vigorous plants room to reach up to six feet. It tolerates heat and poor soil well and blooms nonstop from midsummer until frost. Deadhead to keep the flush going, and stake in exposed sites since the branching stems can grow heavy.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring where plants will grow; do not transplant.

directions: This California poppy strain glows with deep red-orange, saucer-shaped blooms that open in sun and close at night. Sow seeds directly in early spring where they are to grow, pressing them onto the surface since the taproot resents transplanting. Full sun and lean, well-drained soil suit it best, and it needs little water once established. It self-sows reliably to return and naturalize in gardens and meadows.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; as a biennial it flowers the second year.

directions: Money plant, or silver dollar plant, is grown for its charming purple spring flowers and the translucent, coin-shaped seed pods that follow. Sow seeds shallowly in spring or fall, remembering that as a biennial it forms leaves the first year and blooms the second. It grows well in partial shade with average moisture and self-sows to keep returning. Leave the pods to dry on the plant, then peel the papery outer layers for silvery dried arrangements.

planting.note: Fall sowing or cold stratification improves germination of this native seed.

directions: This prairie native coneflower carries purple-pink blooms with drooping petals around a spiny central cone from early to midsummer. The seed germinates best after cold, so sow outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where its deep taproot makes it very drought tolerant once established. It supports bees and butterflies, and its roots have a long history of medicinal use.

planting.note: Fall sow for cold stratification; spring sowing works after chilling seed.

directions: New England aster is a tall native perennial that bursts into pink-to-purple blooms from late summer into fall when many flowers have faded. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing, and give plants full sun with room to reach several feet tall. It is a vital late-season nectar source for migrating monarchs and other pollinators. Pinch stems in early summer for bushier growth, and divide clumps every few years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Fall sow for natural cold stratification; spring sow after chilling.

directions: Ohio spiderwort is a hardy native perennial with grassy foliage and clusters of blue to lavender three-petaled flowers that open fresh each morning. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing for best germination. It adapts to full sun or partial shade and average to moist soil, spreading gently to form clumps. Shear back after the first bloom flush to tidy the plant and encourage a second round of flowers.

planting.note: Surface sow in cool spring or fall where plants are to grow; needs light and cool soil.

directions: Oriental poppy is a long-lived perennial famous for its huge, silky scarlet blooms with dark purple-black centers in late spring. Surface-sow the fine seeds in cool spring or fall directly where they are to grow, since poppies dislike transplanting and germinate best in cool soil with light. Give full sun and well-drained ground, and expect the foliage to die back after flowering as the plant goes summer dormant. Mark the location so you do not disturb the crown, which resprouts in fall.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring in cool soil.

directions: This tricolor painted daisy opens bold three-inch blooms with concentric rings of white, pink, red, or yellow around a dark bullseye center. Sow seeds directly in early spring in cool soil, covering lightly and thinning seedlings once they establish. Grow in full sun with average moisture, and stake if plants grow lanky in rich soil. Deadhead to extend the display and cut stems for cheerful, long-lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Fall sowing or cold stratification improves germination of this native seed.

directions: Pale purple coneflower is a native prairie perennial with elegant, drooping lavender-pink petals around a tall spiky cone. Sow seed outdoors in fall or cold-stratify before spring sowing to break dormancy. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, where the deep taproot makes it very drought tolerant once established. It draws bees and butterflies in bloom and finches to the seed heads, which can be left standing for winter interest.
